app.title: ENIAC — Decimal Arithmetic with Vacuum Tubes
app.subtitle: How the first general-purpose computer did math before binary took over (1945)
triode.intro: Before transistors, vacuum tubes were the only electronic switches. ENIAC used 17,468 of them. Each triode works like a gate valve: the grid voltage controls whether electrons flow from cathode to plate. Positive grid = conducting (ON). Negative grid = blocked (OFF).
triode.title: The Triode — A Vacuum Tube as a Digital Switch
triode.ariaLabel: Triode vacuum tube acting as a digital switch
triode.insight: This is the same principle as a MOSFET transistor — a small control signal (grid voltage / gate voltage) switches a larger current on or off. The triode is just 1000x bigger, 1000x slower, and 1000x more power-hungry. ENIAC needed 150 kilowatts to run.
ring.intro: ENIAC didn't use binary. Each decimal digit (0-9) was stored in a ring of 10 vacuum tubes — exactly one tube is 'on' at any time, and its position in the ring tells you the digit. Sending a pulse advances the on-position by one step. When it wraps from 9 to 0, it generates a carry to the next digit.
ring.title: Decade Ring Counter — One Decimal Digit
ring.ariaLabel: Decade ring counter with 10 vacuum tubes
ring.insight: This is how ENIAC stored one decimal digit. 10 vacuum tubes for one digit — vs. 4 binary flip-flops in a binary system. Decimal is intuitive for humans but uses 3x more hardware than necessary.
acc.intro: An ENIAC accumulator chains multiple ring counters together to store multi-digit numbers. To add, it sends pulses into each digit's ring counter — when a ring wraps from 9 to 0, the carry pulse flows to the next digit. This is addition by counting, not by logic gates.
acc.title: ENIAC Accumulator — Decimal Addition
acc.insight: A real ENIAC accumulator had 10 decades (10-digit numbers) and used ~550 vacuum tubes. ENIAC had 20 of these accumulators — over 11,000 tubes just for number storage and addition.
comp.intro: ENIAC's decimal approach and modern binary produce the same results but use fundamentally different methods. Decimal uses pulse counting through ring counters; binary uses XOR/AND gate logic through adders. Binary needs far fewer components but was less intuitive in 1945.
comp.title: ENIAC (Decimal) vs Modern (Binary)
comp.ariaLabel: Side-by-side comparison of decimal and binary addition
comp.insight: ENIAC's designers chose decimal because it was familiar and easy to interface with punch cards. The binary approach used by every modern computer is 3x more efficient in hardware but was less intuitive in 1945. John von Neumann's EDVAC (1949) switched to binary, and the world never looked back.
footer.credit: Built with vacuum tube and gate-level arithmetic models
